description: The salt sits beyond a child's reach until one hand draws it close. 'pilu' is an agent's active taking of something into immediate control or use. 'howela' (receive) can happen without that action, and 'kelomi' (accept) concerns willing uptake rather than the practical taking itself.
articulatory_notes: Close the lips for unvoiced 'p' and release them into stressed high-front unrounded 'i'. Touch the tongue tip to the alveolar ridge for voiced 'l', let air pass along its sides, then raise the back of the tongue and round the lips for high-back 'u'.
usage_notes: The taken person or thing is the direct object, and the event-noun rule supplies an act of taking. Consent and authority remain separate relations, so the same verb can report a welcomed taking or a disputed one. 'phelu' (hold) describes the continuing relation after something has been taken.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Because 'pilu' does not smuggle permission into the verb, a text can name the taking and then ask whose consent or authority was present.

semantic_domains.activity: actively bringing something into immediate control or use
semantic_domains.physical: taking hold of a person or material object
